findings.title: DOUBLE PRECISION used for HR-sensitive hours/days columns — rounding errors accumulate
findings.code_evidence:
"estimatedHours" DOUBLE PRECISION,
    "progress" INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
...
    "hours" DOUBLE PRECISION NOT NULL,
...
    "days" DOUBLE PRECISION NOT NULL,
findings.description: Three columns critical to HR/payroll computations — tasks.estimatedHours, time_entries.hours, and leaves.days — are declared DOUBLE PRECISION (IEEE 754 binary float). Binary floats cannot represent most decimal fractions exactly (e.g. 0.1 + 0.2 ≠ 0.3). For leave-day balances and time-entry aggregations, floating-point accumulation produces incorrect totals that silently diverge from true decimal values.
findings.root_cause: Prisma Float maps to DOUBLE PRECISION by default; no explicit @db.Decimal annotation was applied to HR-sensitive numeric fields.
findings.impact: Accumulated rounding errors in leave balance calculations, time entry aggregations, and project hour reporting; payroll/audit discrepancies possible at scale.
findings.suggested_fix: Convert to NUMERIC: ALTER TABLE time_entries ALTER COLUMN hours TYPE NUMERIC(5,2) USING hours::numeric(5,2); same for leaves.days NUMERIC(6,2) and tasks.estimatedHours NUMERIC(5,2). Fixed by later migration 20260524100100_dat005_convert_float_to_decimal.
acceptance_criteria: SELECT 0.1::double precision + 0.2::double precision = 0.3 returns false; SELECT 0.1::numeric(5,2) + 0.2::numeric(5,2) = 0.3 returns true
All three columns are NUMERIC after migration
findings.notes: Fixed by migration 20260524100100_dat005_convert_float_to_decimal (2026-05-24). Issue existed from init (2025-11-16) to that date.

findings.title: leave_balances.totalDays declared DOUBLE PRECISION — balance summaries accumulate float errors
findings.description: leave_balances.totalDays is declared DOUBLE PRECISION. This column aggregates per-user annual leave allocations and carryovers. Float arithmetic on day counts can produce off-by-epsilon results that surface as fractional days when compared to integer day-count expectations in HR reporting.
findings.root_cause: Prisma Float maps to DOUBLE PRECISION by default; no explicit @db.Decimal annotation was applied.
findings.impact: HR balance reports may show residual fractions (e.g., 24.999999999999996 days instead of 25), causing off-by-one bugs in leave approval gating.
findings.suggested_fix: ALTER TABLE leave_balances ALTER COLUMN totalDays TYPE NUMERIC(6,2) USING totalDays::numeric(6,2). Fixed by later migration 20260524100100.
acceptance_criteria: leave_balances.totalDays column type is NUMERIC(6,2) in the final schema

findings.title: ON DELETE CASCADE on time_entries.userId destroys labor history when user is deleted
findings.code_evidence: ALTER TABLE "time_entries" ADD CONSTRAINT "time_entries_userId_fkey" FOREIGN KEY ("userId") REFERENCES "users"("id") ON DELETE CASCADE ON UPDATE CASCADE;
findings.description: Deleting a user triggers CASCADE deletion of all their time_entries rows. Time entries are labor records used for project billing, government auditing, and resource planning. In French public sector context (DSI), these records may need to be retained for statutory audit periods (5-10 years) even after an employee leaves. The CASCADE makes this impossible without an explicit pre-deletion backup step.
findings.root_cause: Convenience CASCADE applied during initial schema design without considering HR data retention obligations.
findings.impact: Hard-deleting a user account permanently erases all their logged work hours, breaking project billing reconciliation and historical analytics.
findings.suggested_fix: Change to ON DELETE RESTRICT or ON DELETE SET NULL (with userId nullable) depending on chosen retention policy. If full-erasure is desired, document the explicit operator decision.
acceptance_criteria: Deleting a user with time entries either raises an error (RESTRICT) or nulls the userId field (SET NULL)
Time entry rows are preserved after user deletion
findings.notes: Later migration 20260604103344_dat008_026_user_fk_full_erasure documents an explicit operator decision: this app is NOT the system of legal record, so full erasure is intentional. Issue is resolved by documented design decision.

findings.title: ON DELETE CASCADE on leaves.userId destroys leave history when user is deleted
findings.code_evidence: ALTER TABLE "leaves" ADD CONSTRAINT "leaves_userId_fkey" FOREIGN KEY ("userId") REFERENCES "users"("id") ON DELETE CASCADE ON UPDATE CASCADE;
findings.description: Deleting a user cascades to delete all their leave records. Leave records represent approved/rejected absence decisions with statutory significance in French public sector HR. Cascade deletion makes it impossible to reconstruct approved leave history for audit, payroll correction, or labour dispute resolution after an employee departure.
findings.root_cause: Convenience CASCADE applied during initial schema design without considering HR data retention obligations.
findings.impact: All leave records (approved, rejected, pending) are silently deleted when a user account is hard-deleted, preventing post-departure HR audits.
findings.suggested_fix: Change to ON DELETE RESTRICT or implement soft-delete with anonymisation. If full-erasure is the explicit policy, document the decision in a migration comment.
acceptance_criteria: Deleting a user with leave records either raises an error (RESTRICT) or preserves anonymised records
findings.notes: Later migration 20260604103344_dat008_026_user_fk_full_erasure documents an explicit operator decision: full erasure is intentional and this app is not the SIRH of legal record. Issue is resolved by documented design decision.

findings.title: ON DELETE CASCADE on leave_balances.leaveTypeId destroys balance history when leave type is deleted
findings.code_evidence: ALTER TABLE "leave_balances" ADD CONSTRAINT "leave_balances_leaveTypeId_fkey" FOREIGN KEY ("leaveTypeId") REFERENCES "leave_type_configs"("id") ON DELETE CASCADE ON UPDATE CASCADE;
findings.description: Deleting a leave_type_config cascades to delete all leave_balances rows for that type across all users and years. This means retiring or removing a leave type (e.g., abolishing RTT during a reform) silently erases the historical annual allocations for all employees, making it impossible to verify past entitlements. The ON DELETE RESTRICT or a soft-delete on leave_type_configs would prevent accidental data destruction.
findings.root_cause: CASCADE chosen for referential convenience without considering that leave_type_configs rows represent system reference data that should be archived, not deleted.
findings.impact: Deleting a leave type destroys all historical annual balance records for all users, breaking audit trails for leave entitlement verification.
findings.suggested_fix: Change leave_balances.leaveTypeId FK to ON DELETE RESTRICT. Add isActive flag to leave_type_configs (already present) and use deactivation instead of deletion.
acceptance_criteria: Attempting to DELETE a leave_type_configs row that has associated leave_balances rows raises a foreign key violation
Leave type deactivation uses isActive=false, not DELETE
findings.notes: No later migration changes this CASCADE. Remains open.

findings.title: ON DELETE CASCADE on project_snapshots destroys historical trend data when project is hard-deleted
findings.code_evidence: ALTER TABLE "project_snapshots" ADD CONSTRAINT "project_snapshots_projectId_fkey" FOREIGN KEY ("projectId") REFERENCES "projects"("id") ON DELETE CASCADE ON UPDATE CASCADE;
findings.description: project_snapshots is an append-only time-series table recording project progress over time. With CASCADE, hard-deleting a project destroys all its snapshot history, removing the ability to reconstruct project velocity metrics, detect pattern anomalies, and satisfy retrospective reporting requirements. Snapshots have no independently meaningful identity without the project, but retain analytical value beyond the project lifecycle.
findings.root_cause: Default cascade behavior applied to time-series child table without considering post-project-lifecycle reporting needs.
findings.impact: Hard-deleting a project permanently erases all progress snapshots, making historical dashboards impossible to reconstruct.
findings.suggested_fix: Change to ON DELETE RESTRICT and require project archival (status=CANCELLED/COMPLETED) before allowing hard deletion. Fixed by later migration 20260525200000_dat007_project_fk_restrict_preserve_history.
acceptance_criteria: Attempting to DELETE a project with snapshots raises a foreign key violation
ProjectsService.checkProjectDependencies surfaces this as ConflictException
findings.notes: Fixed by migration 20260525200000_dat007_project_fk_restrict_preserve_history which changes to ON DELETE RESTRICT.

findings.title: leave_balances unique index on nullable userId treats multiple NULL rows as distinct — global balance uniqueness not enforced
findings.description: userId is nullable (TEXT without NOT NULL), representing a 'global' balance row when NULL. Postgres NULLS DISTINCT semantics mean multiple rows with userId=NULL for the same (leaveTypeId, year) all satisfy the unique constraint. A concurrent upsertBalance operation can therefore race-create two global balance rows for the same (leaveTypeId, year), making resolveAllocatedDays non-deterministic.
findings.root_cause: Standard B-tree unique index treats each NULL as not equal to any other NULL (ISO SQL semantics), so the uniqueness guarantee does not hold for global (userId=NULL) rows.
findings.impact: Race conditions in concurrent leave balance upserts can create duplicate global balance rows, causing non-deterministic behavior in leave allocation resolution.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a partial unique index: CREATE UNIQUE INDEX leave_balances_global_unique ON leave_balances (leaveTypeId, year) WHERE userId IS NULL. Fixed by later migration 20260523171000_self_approved_and_global_balance_unique.
acceptance_criteria: Two concurrent INSERTs with userId=NULL, same leaveTypeId and year result in a unique constraint violation on the second INSERT
Partial index leave_balances_global_unique exists in pg_indexes
findings.verification_command: psql -c "SELECT indexname FROM pg_indexes WHERE tablename='leave_balances' AND indexname='leave_balances_global_unique';"
findings.notes: Fixed by migration 20260523171000_self_approved_and_global_balance_unique (2026-05-23) which adds the partial unique index.

findings.title: tasks.progress and epics.progress have no CHECK BETWEEN 0 AND 100 — negative or >100 values accepted
findings.code_evidence:
"progress" INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
    "startDate" TIMESTAMP(3),
    "endDate" TIMESTAMP(3),
    "createdAt" TIMESTAMP(3) NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    "updatedAt" TIMESTAMP(3) NOT NULL,

    CONSTRAINT "epics_pkey" PRIMARY KEY ("id")
...
    "progress" INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
findings.description: Both epics.progress and tasks.progress are INTEGER columns with no CHECK constraint. Values outside [0, 100] are silently accepted at the DB level. While DTO validation exists at the application layer, a buggy service path or direct SQL write can store progress=150 or progress=-5, corrupting dashboards and analytics that assume a percentage.
findings.root_cause: Prisma does not support CHECK constraints in its DSL; hand-authored CHECK migration was not added alongside the column definition.
findings.impact: Invalid progress values corrupt project completion dashboards and milestone tracking without any database-level rejection.
findings.suggested_fix: ALTER TABLE tasks ADD CONSTRAINT tasks_progress_ck CHECK (progress BETWEEN 0 AND 100); same for epics. Fixed by later migration 20260527120000_dat003_dat004_business_invariants.
acceptance_criteria: INSERT INTO tasks (progress) VALUES (101) raises check constraint violation
INSERT INTO epics (progress) VALUES (-1) raises check constraint violation
findings.verification_command: psql -c "SELECT conname FROM pg_constraint WHERE conrelid='tasks'::regclass AND contype='c' AND conname='tasks_progress_ck';"

findings.title: project_members.allocation has no CHECK BETWEEN 0 AND 100 — negative or impossible percentages accepted
findings.code_evidence:
"allocation" INTEGER,
    "startDate" TIMESTAMP(3),
    "endDate" TIMESTAMP(3),
    "createdAt" TIMESTAMP(3) NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,

    CONSTRAINT "project_members_pkey" PRIMARY KEY ("id")
findings.description: project_members.allocation represents a percentage allocation of a team member's time to a project. No CHECK constraint enforces the [0, 100] range. Values like allocation=200 or allocation=-10 are silently accepted, producing misleading capacity planning data.
findings.root_cause: No database-level constraint enforcing the percentage domain; only application-layer DTO validation guards this.
findings.impact: Invalid allocation values corrupt resource capacity reports and project staffing calculations.
findings.suggested_fix: ALTER TABLE project_members ADD CONSTRAINT project_members_allocation_ck CHECK (allocation BETWEEN 0 AND 100). Fixed by later migration 20260527120000_dat003_dat004_business_invariants.
acceptance_criteria: INSERT INTO project_members (allocation) VALUES (101) raises check constraint violation
findings.verification_command: psql -c "SELECT conname FROM pg_constraint WHERE conrelid='project_members'::regclass AND contype='c';"

findings.title: time_entries.hours has no CHECK for positive value or daily cap — zero and negative entries accepted
findings.code_evidence:
"hours" DOUBLE PRECISION NOT NULL,
    "description" TEXT,
    "activityType" "ActivityType" NOT NULL,
    "createdAt" TIMESTAMP(3) NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    "updatedAt" TIMESTAMP(3) NOT NULL,

    CONSTRAINT "time_entries_pkey" PRIMARY KEY ("id")
findings.description: time_entries.hours is NOT NULL but has no CHECK constraint. Negative hours (e.g., -2.0) or absurdly large values (e.g., 1000) are accepted. The DTO layer enforces @Min(0.25) @Max(24) but a buggy service path bypasses this. A row with hours=-5 silently reduces project hour totals and causes incorrect billing calculations.
findings.root_cause: No CHECK constraint at the DB level; only DTO validation at the application boundary.
findings.impact: Negative or out-of-range time entries corrupt project billing totals and resource utilization reports.
findings.suggested_fix: ALTER TABLE time_entries ADD CONSTRAINT time_entries_hours_ck CHECK (hours >= 0 AND hours <= 24). Fixed by later migration 20260528120000_dat032_dat033_position_and_hours_bounds.
acceptance_criteria: INSERT INTO time_entries (hours) VALUES (-1) raises check constraint violation
INSERT INTO time_entries (hours) VALUES (25) raises check constraint violation
findings.verification_command: psql -c "SELECT conname FROM pg_constraint WHERE conrelid='time_entries'::regclass AND contype='c' AND conname='time_entries_hours_ck';"

findings.title: leaves.days has no CHECK > 0 — zero-day and negative leave records accepted
findings.code_evidence:
"days" DOUBLE PRECISION NOT NULL,
    "status" "LeaveStatus" NOT NULL DEFAULT 'APPROVED',
    "comment" TEXT,
    "createdAt" TIMESTAMP(3) NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    "updatedAt" TIMESTAMP(3) NOT NULL,

    CONSTRAINT "leaves_pkey" PRIMARY KEY ("id")
findings.description: leaves.days is NOT NULL but has no CHECK constraint. A zero or negative day count is a logical impossibility for a leave request. Without this guard, a buggy or malicious caller could create a leave with days=0 (a no-op that still blocks approval queues) or days=-5 (that would inflate computed leave balances).
findings.root_cause: No CHECK at the DB level enforcing the business invariant that a leave duration must be strictly positive.
findings.impact: Zero or negative leave day records corrupt HR balance accounting and approval workflow logic.
findings.suggested_fix: ALTER TABLE leaves ADD CONSTRAINT leaves_days_ck CHECK (days > 0). Fixed by later migration 20260527120000_dat003_dat004_business_invariants.
acceptance_criteria: INSERT INTO leaves (days) VALUES (0) raises check constraint violation
INSERT INTO leaves (days) VALUES (-1) raises check constraint violation
findings.verification_command: psql -c "SELECT conname FROM pg_constraint WHERE conrelid='leaves'::regclass AND contype='c' AND conname='leaves_days_ck';"

findings.title: telework_recurring_rules.dayOfWeek and predefined_task_recurring_rules.dayOfWeek have no CHECK BETWEEN 0 AND 6
findings.code_evidence:
"dayOfWeek" INTEGER NOT NULL,
    "startDate" TIMESTAMP(3) NOT NULL,
    "endDate" TIMESTAMP(3),
    "isActive" BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT true,
    "createdById" TEXT NOT NULL,
...
    "dayOfWeek" INTEGER NOT NULL,
    "period" TEXT NOT NULL,
findings.description: Both telework_recurring_rules.dayOfWeek and predefined_task_recurring_rules.dayOfWeek store a day-of-week as INTEGER. No CHECK constraint ensures the value is in [0, 6] (Sunday=0 to Saturday=6). A value of 7 or -1 would silently corrupt the recurring schedule logic without any database-level rejection. No later migration adds this constraint.
findings.root_cause: Day-of-week semantics encoded as bare INTEGER without domain enforcement; no CHECK added in any subsequent migration.
findings.impact: Out-of-range day-of-week values corrupt recurring schedule generation, producing schedules that never expand or expand incorrectly.
findings.suggested_fix: ALTER TABLE telework_recurring_rules ADD CONSTRAINT telework_recurring_rules_dayofweek_ck CHECK ("dayOfWeek" BETWEEN 0 AND 6); ALTER TABLE predefined_task_recurring_rules ADD CONSTRAINT predefined_task_recurring_rules_dayofweek_ck CHECK ("dayOfWeek" BETWEEN 0 AND 6);
acceptance_criteria: INSERT INTO telework_recurring_rules (dayOfWeek) VALUES (7) raises check constraint violation
INSERT INTO predefined_task_recurring_rules (dayOfWeek) VALUES (-1) raises check constraint violation
findings.verification_command: psql -c "SELECT conname FROM pg_constraint WHERE conrelid='telework_recurring_rules'::regclass AND contype='c';"
findings.notes: No later migration adds this CHECK. Remains open as of the latest audited migration.

findings.title: events.recurrenceDay has no CHECK constraint — invalid day-of-week/month values silently accepted
findings.description: events.recurrenceDay is a nullable INTEGER representing a day-of-week or day-of-month for recurring event logic. No CHECK constraint bounds the value. A value of 32 or -1 would silently corrupt recurring event expansion. Similarly, recurrenceWeekInterval has no CHECK >= 1 constraint, allowing zero or negative intervals that would create infinite or backward-expanding recurrences.
findings.root_cause: Recurrence columns added as bare INTEGERs without domain constraints; only application-layer validation guards these.
findings.impact: Out-of-range recurrenceDay or invalid recurrenceWeekInterval values cause recurring event expansion bugs, potentially producing unbounded loops or empty event sets.
findings.suggested_fix: ALTER TABLE events ADD CONSTRAINT events_recurrenceDay_ck CHECK ("recurrenceDay" BETWEEN 0 AND 6); ALTER TABLE events ADD CONSTRAINT events_recurrenceWeekInterval_ck CHECK ("recurrenceWeekInterval" >= 1);
acceptance_criteria: INSERT into events with recurrenceDay=8 raises constraint violation
INSERT into events with recurrenceWeekInterval=0 raises constraint violation
findings.verification_command: psql -c "SELECT conname FROM pg_constraint WHERE conrelid='events'::regclass AND contype='c';"
findings.notes: No later migration adds these CHECKs. Remains open. Confidence medium because the semantic range of recurrenceDay (0-6 vs 1-31) depends on application logic not fully verifiable from migrations alone.

findings.title: tasks.startTime, tasks.endTime, events.startTime, events.endTime stored as TEXT without format validation at DB level
findings.description: startTime and endTime for tasks and events are stored as TEXT. No CHECK constraint enforces HH:MM format at the database level. Values like '25:99', '9:5', or empty strings are silently accepted. This allows malformed time values to enter the database and corrupt planning, export, and scheduling features that rely on string comparison and parsing.
findings.root_cause: Using TEXT for time-of-day is a reasonable choice to avoid Prisma DateTime overhead, but without a format CHECK constraint the DB accepts any string.
findings.impact: Malformed time values corrupt planning displays, calendar exports, and any feature performing time arithmetic via split(':').
findings.suggested_fix: ADD CONSTRAINT tasks_startTime_format_ck CHECK (startTime ~ '^([0-1]?[0-9]|2[0-3]):[0-5][0-9]$'); same for endTime. Fixed by later migration 20260527140000_dat013_time_format_check.
acceptance_criteria: INSERT INTO tasks (startTime) VALUES ('25:00') raises check constraint violation
INSERT INTO tasks (startTime) VALUES ('9:5') raises check constraint violation
findings.verification_command: psql -c "SELECT conname FROM pg_constraint WHERE conrelid='tasks'::regclass AND contype='c' AND conname LIKE '%time%';"

findings.title: predefined_tasks.defaultDuration and predefined_task_assignments.period stored as TEXT — any string accepted
findings.code_evidence:
"defaultDuration" TEXT NOT NULL,
    "isActive" BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT true,
    "createdById" TEXT NOT NULL,
...
    "period" TEXT NOT NULL,
    "assignedById" TEXT NOT NULL,
findings.description: defaultDuration and period are TEXT columns intended to hold closed value sets ('HALF_DAY', 'FULL_DAY', 'TIME_SLOT' and 'MORNING', 'AFTERNOON', 'FULL_DAY' respectively). Without a CHECK constraint or enum type, any string is accepted. A typo like 'HALF_DAI' or 'FULLDAY' would silently corrupt assignment scheduling logic.
findings.root_cause: Closed string value sets were implemented as TEXT without enum type or CHECK constraint; Prisma does not enforce string enums at the DB level.
findings.impact: Invalid period or defaultDuration values corrupt predefined task scheduling; the application silently processes unexpected strings.
findings.suggested_fix: Promote to native PostgreSQL enum types or add CHECK constraints. Fixed by later migration 20260527130000_dat012_promote_string_enums which converts these to proper enums.
acceptance_criteria: INSERT INTO predefined_tasks (defaultDuration) VALUES ('INVALID') raises type error or check violation
INSERT INTO predefined_task_assignments (period) VALUES ('INVALID') raises type error or check violation

findings.title: password_reset_tokens.createdById CASCADE — deleting admin who created token cascades to delete active token
findings.code_evidence: ALTER TABLE "password_reset_tokens" ADD CONSTRAINT "password_reset_tokens_createdById_fkey" FOREIGN KEY ("createdById") REFERENCES "users"("id") ON DELETE CASCADE ON UPDATE CASCADE;
findings.description: The createdById FK on password_reset_tokens cascades on delete. If an admin who created a password reset token is themselves deleted before the token is used, the cascade silently deletes the token, causing the password reset link to return 'invalid token' without any explanation. This is a user-facing data loss scenario.
findings.root_cause: Cascade chosen for convenience without considering the lifecycle mismatch between the creator (admin) and the artifact (password reset token).
findings.impact: An in-flight password reset for a user fails silently if the admin who created it is deleted before the user clicks the link.
findings.suggested_fix: Change to ON DELETE SET NULL (with createdById nullable) or ON DELETE RESTRICT. Fixed by later migration 20260604103344_dat008_026_user_fk_full_erasure which changes to SET NULL.
acceptance_criteria: Deleting an admin user does not delete their issued password_reset_tokens rows
password_reset_tokens.createdById is set to NULL when the creator is deleted
findings.notes: Fixed by migration 20260604103344_dat008_026_user_fk_full_erasure (2026-06-04) which changes to SET NULL with nullable column.

20251116093059_init: user_services composite unique (userId, serviceId) prevents duplicate memberships
20251116093059_init: project_members composite unique (projectId, userId) prevents duplicate memberships
20251116093059_init: task_dependencies composite unique (taskId, dependsOnTaskId) prevents duplicate dependency edges
20251116093059_init: task_raci composite unique (taskId, userId, role) prevents duplicate RACI assignments
20251116093059_init: telework_schedules composite unique (userId, date) prevents duplicate entries per day
20251116093059_init: skills.name unique index prevents duplicate skill definitions
20251116093059_init: all FK relationships use appropriate ON DELETE SET NULL for nullable optional references (departmentId, epicId, milestoneId, assigneeId)
20251116093059_init: migration wrapped in standard Prisma-generated DDL — appears transactional

20251215120000_add_missing_tables: data migration correctly handles NULL leave_type_id by defaulting to CP type (line 92)
20251215120000_add_missing_tables: NOT NULL migration pattern (add nullable → populate → set NOT NULL) is correct and safe
20251225160000_add_task_assignees: task_assignees composite unique (taskId, userId) prevents duplicate assignments
20251225160000_add_task_assignees: appropriate CASCADE on both FKs — task assignee list is operational data
20260104102501_add_holidays_and_task_fields: holidays.date unique index prevents duplicate holiday entries per date
20260104102501_add_holidays_and_task_fields: holidays.createdById uses ON DELETE RESTRICT — preserves creator accountability

20260214192223_add_chef_projet_events_ext_intervention: events.projectId uses ON DELETE SET NULL — events survive project deletion

20260223094815_add_visible_statuses: visibleStatuses TEXT[] default empty array — no integrity risk

20260321112607_add_predefined_tasks_telework_recurring_password_reset: predefined_task_assignments composite unique prevents duplicate assignments per task/user/date/period
